core_takeaway: Agent_1 used the first turn to make Pencil focal and secured it, but consensus formed around Baseline Option B, giving every agent a salient item and giving Agent_5 Quill worth 35, which beat Agent_1's Pencil worth 33. The transcript shows anchoring without a decisive first-speaker advantage.
why_or_counterexample: Agent_1's early anchor kept Pencil, but Agent_5's late position still locked its top item Quill and the final accepted proposal was Agent_5's identical baseline. A more Agent_1-favorable proposal that added Quill to Agent_1 was rejected unanimously, so the process limited first-position leverage.

phase_notes.public_discussion: Agent_1 opened by claiming Pencil and asking others to reveal priorities. The group converged on Baseline Option B, and Agent_5 explicitly backed it because it gave Agent_5 Quill, its highest-value item.
phase_notes.private_thinking: Private plans treated Baseline B as the dominant consensus path: keep Pencil with Agent_1, distribute the other focal items, and defer Jewel/Quill conflicts to later compensation. Agent_5's private plan centered on defending Quill while preserving consensus.
phase_notes.formal_proposals: Four of five structured proposals were the same Baseline B allocation. Agent_2's alternative gave Agent_1 Pencil plus Quill and concentrated the rest with Agent_2, but it received no support.
phase_notes.private_voting: Agents accepted baseline proposals because their own immediate allocation was good: Agent_3 got Jewel, Agent_4 got Stone, Agent_5 got Quill. Proposal #2 failed because it zeroed out several agents despite improving Agent_1.
phase_notes.reflection: No reflection entries are present in this transcript.

mechanisms.theme: Opening anchor secured Pencil but only that item
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1's first-speaker move made Pencil the focal claim, and later discussion repeatedly treated Pencil with Agent_1 as fixed. This is a process advantage, but it translated only into Agent_1's 33-point item.

evidence.quote: I agree Pencil stays with you in Round 1.

mechanisms.theme: Baseline consensus gave Agent_5 the higher-valued top item
mechanisms.explanation: The shared baseline honored Agent_1's Pencil claim while also giving Agent_5 Quill, which Agent_5 valued at 35. That item-value fact explains why the last position beat the first despite the Pencil anchor.

When an alternative proposal tried to give Agent_1 both Pencil and Quill, it was rejected by every voter. The bargaining process constrained first-position gains rather than letting Agent_1 expand the opening anchor.

evidence.quote: Gives me nothing; zero utility in Round 1; not favorable.

mechanisms.theme: Private votes show self-interest, not deference to first speaker
mechanisms.explanation: Agents accepted identical baseline proposals because each protected its own high-priority allocation. Agent_5's votes explicitly cite Quill's 35-point value, so the last-position payoff came from accepted self-interest within the consensus.

evidence.quote: Gives me Quill (35); highest immediate utility in Round 1.

mechanisms.theme: Final accepted proposal came from the last agent's copy
mechanisms.explanation: Although Proposal #1 was also unanimously acceptable, the tabulation records Proposal #5 as the unanimous consensus. This is a direct counterexample to any strong procedural advantage for the first proposal or first speaker.
